titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 3 December 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/89

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
3 Dec My x the p r Such a nice long letter form p r last night quite bucked
up the b w , & as it was dated Tuesday there ought to be an
accumulation of 3 extra letters en route. Letters come in a most
erratic way, & it may be the Xmas disorganisation already felt. Would you let Sir E know that I think the brewing
troubles between our men & the Nationalists seem to have
blown over & I have every hope there will be no trouble
unless J.R makes another visit or speech of a provocative nature.
This caution especially applies to Xmas when our men will
be a little on their metal. Would you let Gen' P know that Gen H P is leaving
this week or next & is expecting an appointment at home in
a higher rank. If he is given one & not Gen P it will be
one of the most disgraceful things possible. He has not been
fit either physically (or mentally owing to physical causes)
since he came out, & there can be no comparison in the
relative value of the 2 men. If he is supposed to have had
practical experience out here, I can only state that Gen P saw
more in his 2 days than the other in his 2 months. I should
like Gen P to be told this frankly.
I had a 75 motor drive last
night,& ought to have had
a violent end. We stopped to let some waggons go bye, & started
again, when White, who luckily was driving us thought
something was wrong & pulled up, we we found our 2 front wheels
had become uncoupled ! If we had been moving fast ! ! I see in today's gazette that the man who succeeded me
at the War Office & who has been there ever since has been made
a brevet Lieut Colonel. He has no war experience & was considered
rather a rotter! Please give my best Salaams to Sir George & & Lady
R & to all our old friends whom you may meet In view of what we have given I think you may have
your travelling exes paid if they again press it, but not otherwise, i e do not
remind them or put them down. I am writing a home letter, & also to Sir Edward to
let him know that Comyn Maim & Ryan are all good chaps,
in case your remark to Lady C may do them mischief. I watch the wind now with the greatest interest,
poor little p r — peagreen reptile I fear — ! But I think it
plucky of you & that you have done the right thing. I do like to think of you with all the others, but how long
you will manage not to take sides I don't know, in spite of
all your cleverness Your B W WBSpender
